question:
Problem B-2
Determine the minimum value of
\[
(r-1)^{2}+\left(\frac{s}{r}-1\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{t}{s}-1\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{4}{t}-1\right)^{2}
\]
for all real numbers \( r, s, t \) with \( 1 \leq r \leq s \leq t \leq 4 \).
solution:
B-2.
First we let \( 0<a<b \) and seek the \( x \) that minimizes
\[
f(x)=\left(\frac{x}{a}-1\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{b}{x}-1\right)^{2} \text { on } a \leq x \leq b
\]

Let \( x / a=z \) and \( b / a=c \). Then
\[
f(x)=g(z)=(z-1)^{2}+\left(\frac{c}{z}-1\right)^{2}
\]

Now \( g^{\prime}(z)=0 \) implies
\[
z^{4}-z^{3}+c z-c^{2}=\left(z^{2}-c\right)\left(z^{2}-z+c\right)=0
\]
the only positive solution is \( z=\sqrt{c} \). Since \( 0<a<b, c>1, \sqrt{c}>1 \), and
\[
g(1)=g(c)=(c-1)^{2}=(\sqrt{c}-1)^{2}(\sqrt{c}+1)^{2}>2(\sqrt{c}-1)^{2}=g(\sqrt{c})
\]

Hence the minimum of \( g(z) \) on \( 1 \leq z \leq c \) occurs at \( z=\sqrt{c} \). It follows that the minimum for \( f(x) \) on \( a \leq x \leq b \) occurs at \( x=a \sqrt{b / a}=\sqrt{a b} \). Then the minimum for the given function of \( r, s, t \) occurs with \( r=\sqrt{s}, t=\sqrt{4 s}=2 r \), and \( s=\sqrt{r t}=r \sqrt{2} \). These imply that \( r=\sqrt{2}, s=2, t=2 \sqrt{2} \). Thus the desired minimum value is \( 4(\sqrt{2}-1)^{2}=12-8 \sqrt{2} \).

descriptive_long.question:
Problem B-2
Determine the minimum value of
\[
(varradius-1)^{2}+\left(\frac{varscalar}{varradius}-1\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{vartangent}{varscalar}-1\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{4}{vartangent}-1\right)^{2}
\]
for all real numbers \( varradius, varscalar, vartangent \) with \( 1 \leq varradius \leq varscalar \leq vartangent \leq 4 \).
descriptive_long.solution:
B-2.
First we let \( 0<paramanchor<parambravo \) and seek the \( varxenon \) that minimizes
\[
f(varxenon)=\left(\frac{varxenon}{paramanchor}-1\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{parambravo}{varxenon}-1\right)^{2} \text { on } paramanchor \leq varxenon \leq parambravo
\]

Let \( varxenon / paramanchor=varzenith \) and \( parambravo / paramanchor=paramcharlie \). Then
\[
f(varxenon)=g(varzenith)=(varzenith-1)^{2}+\left(\frac{paramcharlie}{varzenith}-1\right)^{2}
\]

Now \( g^{\prime}(varzenith)=0 \) implies
\[
varzenith^{4}-varzenith^{3}+paramcharlie \, varzenith-paramcharlie^{2}=\left(varzenith^{2}-paramcharlie\right)\left(varzenith^{2}-varzenith+paramcharlie\right)=0
\]
the only positive solution is \( varzenith=\sqrt{paramcharlie} \). Since \( 0<paramanchor<parambravo, paramcharlie>1, \sqrt{paramcharlie}>1 \), and
\[
g(1)=g(paramcharlie)=(paramcharlie-1)^{2}=(\sqrt{paramcharlie}-1)^{2}(\sqrt{paramcharlie}+1)^{2}>2(\sqrt{paramcharlie}-1)^{2}=g(\sqrt{paramcharlie})
\]

Hence the minimum of \( g(varzenith) \) on \( 1 \leq varzenith \leq paramcharlie \) occurs at \( varzenith=\sqrt{paramcharlie} \). It follows that the minimum for \( f(varxenon) \) on \( paramanchor \leq varxenon \leq parambravo \) occurs at \( varxenon=paramanchor \sqrt{parambravo / paramanchor}=\sqrt{paramanchor \, parambravo} \). Then the minimum for the given function of \( varradius, varscalar, vartangent \) occurs with \( varradius=\sqrt{varscalar}, vartangent=\sqrt{4 varscalar}=2 varradius \), and \( varscalar=\sqrt{varradius \, vartangent}=varradius \sqrt{2} \). These imply that \( varradius=\sqrt{2}, varscalar=2, vartangent=2 \sqrt{2} \). Thus the desired minimum value is \( 4(\sqrt{2}-1)^{2}=12-8 \sqrt{2} \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem B-2
Determine the minimum value of
\[
(peppermint-1)^{2}+\left(\frac{sandstone}{peppermint}-1\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{firebrick}{sandstone}-1\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{4}{firebrick}-1\right)^{2}
\]
for all real numbers \( peppermint, sandstone, firebrick \) with \( 1 \leq peppermint \leq sandstone \leq firebrick \leq 4 \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-2.
First we let \( 0<rainstorm<moonlight \) and seek the \( toadflax \) that minimizes
\[
f(toadflax)=\left(\frac{toadflax}{rainstorm}-1\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{moonlight}{toadflax}-1\right)^{2} \text { on } rainstorm \leq toadflax \leq moonlight
\]

Let \( toadflax / rainstorm=nightshade \) and \( moonlight / rainstorm=driftwood \). Then
\[
f(toadflax)=g(nightshade)=(nightshade-1)^{2}+\left(\frac{driftwood}{nightshade}-1\right)^{2}
\]

Now \( g^{\prime}(nightshade)=0 \) implies
\[
nightshade^{4}-nightshade^{3}+driftwood\, nightshade-driftwood^{2}=\left(nightshade^{2}-driftwood\right)\left(nightshade^{2}-nightshade+driftwood\right)=0
\]
the only positive solution is \( nightshade=\sqrt{driftwood} \). Since \( 0<rainstorm<moonlight, driftwood>1, \sqrt{driftwood}>1 \), and
\[
g(1)=g(driftwood)=(driftwood-1)^{2}=(\sqrt{driftwood}-1)^{2}(\sqrt{driftwood}+1)^{2}>2(\sqrt{driftwood}-1)^{2}=g(\sqrt{driftwood})
\]

Hence the minimum of \( g(nightshade) \) on \( 1 \leq nightshade \leq driftwood \) occurs at \( nightshade=\sqrt{driftwood} \). It follows that the minimum for \( f(toadflax) \) on \( rainstorm \leq toadflax \leq moonlight \) occurs at \( toadflax=rainstorm \sqrt{moonlight / rainstorm}=\sqrt{rainstorm moonlight} \). Then the minimum for the given function of \( peppermint, sandstone, firebrick \) occurs with \( peppermint=\sqrt{sandstone}, firebrick=\sqrt{4 sandstone}=2 peppermint \), and \( sandstone=\sqrt{peppermint firebrick}=peppermint \sqrt{2} \). These imply that \( peppermint=\sqrt{2}, sandstone=2, firebrick=2 \sqrt{2} \). Thus the desired minimum value is \( 4(\sqrt{2}-1)^{2}=12-8 \sqrt{2} \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem B-2
Determine the minimum value of
\[
(fixedvalue-1)^{2}+\left(\frac{steadyvalue}{fixedvalue}-1\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{rigidvalue}{steadyvalue}-1\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{4}{rigidvalue}-1\right)^{2}
\]
for all real numbers \( fixedvalue, steadyvalue, rigidvalue \) with \( 1 \leq fixedvalue \leq steadyvalue \leq rigidvalue \leq 4 \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-2.
First we let \( 0<variabledata<shiftingvalue \) and seek the \( knownnumber \) that minimizes
\[
f(knownnumber)=\left(\frac{knownnumber}{variabledata}-1\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{shiftingvalue}{knownnumber}-1\right)^{2} \text { on } variabledata \leq knownnumber \leq shiftingvalue
\]

Let \( knownnumber / variabledata=certainnum \) and \( shiftingvalue / variabledata=mutablevalue \). Then
\[
f(knownnumber)=g(certainnum)=(certainnum-1)^{2}+\left(\frac{mutablevalue}{certainnum}-1\right)^{2}
\]

Now \( g^{\prime}(certainnum)=0 \) implies
\[
certainnum^{4}-certainnum^{3}+mutablevalue\;certainnum-mutablevalue^{2}=\left(certainnum^{2}-mutablevalue\right)\left(certainnum^{2}-certainnum+mutablevalue\right)=0
\]
the only positive solution is \( certainnum=\sqrt{mutablevalue} \). Since \( 0<variabledata<shiftingvalue, mutablevalue>1, \sqrt{mutablevalue}>1 \), and
\[
g(1)=g(mutablevalue)=(mutablevalue-1)^{2}=(\sqrt{mutablevalue}-1)^{2}(\sqrt{mutablevalue}+1)^{2}>2(\sqrt{mutablevalue}-1)^{2}=g(\sqrt{mutablevalue})
\]

Hence the minimum of \( g(certainnum) \) on \( 1 \leq certainnum \leq mutablevalue \) occurs at \( certainnum=\sqrt{mutablevalue} \). It follows that the minimum for \( f(knownnumber) \) on \( variabledata \leq knownnumber \leq shiftingvalue \) occurs at \( knownnumber=variabledata \sqrt{shiftingvalue / variabledata}=\sqrt{variabledata\;shiftingvalue} \). Then the minimum for the given function of \( fixedvalue, steadyvalue, rigidvalue \) occurs with \( fixedvalue=\sqrt{steadyvalue}, rigidvalue=\sqrt{4\;steadyvalue}=2 fixedvalue \), and \( steadyvalue=\sqrt{fixedvalue\;rigidvalue}=fixedvalue \sqrt{2} \). These imply that \( fixedvalue=\sqrt{2}, steadyvalue=2, rigidvalue=2 \sqrt{2} \). Thus the desired minimum value is \( 4(\sqrt{2}-1)^{2}=12-8 \sqrt{2} \).

garbled_string.question:
Problem B-2
Determine the minimum value of
\[
(qzxwvtnp-1)^{2}+\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp}-1\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{mndplfqe}{hjgrksla}-1\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{4}{mndplfqe}-1\right)^{2}
\]
for all real numbers \( qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mndplfqe \) with \( 1 \leq qzxwvtnp \leq hjgrksla \leq mndplfqe \leq 4 \).
garbled_string.solution:
B-2.
First we let \( 0<bsndkwor<pjqmtecz \) and seek the \( vrcltkse \) that minimizes
\[
f(vrcltkse)=\left(\frac{vrcltkse}{bsndkwor}-1\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{pjqmtecz}{vrcltkse}-1\right)^{2} \text { on } bsndkwor \leq vrcltkse \leq pjqmtecz
\]

Let \( vrcltkse / bsndkwor=gftplrxa \) and \( pjqmtecz / bsndkwor=lzvdfhuy \). Then
\[
f(vrcltkse)=g(gftplrxa)=(gftplrxa-1)^{2}+\left(\frac{lzvdfhuy}{gftplrxa}-1\right)^{2}
\]

Now \( g^{\prime}(gftplrxa)=0 \) implies
\[
gftplrxa^{4}-gftplrxa^{3}+lzvdfhuy\, gftplrxa-lzvdfhuy^{2}=\left(gftplrxa^{2}-lzvdfhuy\right)\left(gftplrxa^{2}-gftplrxa+lzvdfhuy\right)=0
\]
the only positive solution is \( gftplrxa=\sqrt{lzvdfhuy} \). Since \( 0<bsndkwor<pjqmtecz, lzvdfhuy>1, \sqrt{lzvdfhuy}>1 \), and
\[
g(1)=g(lzvdfhuy)=(lzvdfhuy-1)^{2}=(\sqrt{lzvdfhuy}-1)^{2}(\sqrt{lzvdfhuy}+1)^{2}>2(\sqrt{lzvdfhuy}-1)^{2}=g(\sqrt{lzvdfhuy})
\]

Hence the minimum of \( g(gftplrxa) \) on \( 1 \leq gftplrxa \leq lzvdfhuy \) occurs at \( gftplrxa=\sqrt{lzvdfhuy} \). It follows that the minimum for \( f(vrcltkse) \) on \( bsndkwor \leq vrcltkse \leq pjqmtecz \) occurs at \( vrcltkse=bsndkwor \sqrt{pjqmtecz / bsndkwor}=\sqrt{bsndkwor\, pjqmtecz} \). Then the minimum for the given function of \( qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mndplfqe \) occurs with \( qzxwvtnp=\sqrt{hjgrksla}, mndplfqe=\sqrt{4 hjgrksla}=2 qzxwvtnp \), and \( hjgrksla=\sqrt{qzxwvtnp\, mndplfqe}=qzxwvtnp \sqrt{2} \). These imply that \( qzxwvtnp=\sqrt{2}, hjgrksla=2, mndplfqe=2 \sqrt{2} \). Thus the desired minimum value is \( 4(\sqrt{2}-1)^{2}=12-8 \sqrt{2} \).

kernel_variant.solution:
We correct only Steps 4-5 of the draft solution (Steps 1-3 are already
fully justified).  All notation introduced earlier is kept.
In particular  

\[
x_{k}>1\;(0\le k\le 4),\quad
P=x_{0}x_{1}x_{2}x_{3}x_{4},\quad
x_{5}=32/P ,\quad
m=(5,4,3,2,1).
\]

1.  Interior KKT equations.  
With  

\[
A=\frac{32}{P}\Bigl(\frac{32}{P}-1\Bigr),\qquad q=\frac{\mu}{2},
\]

the five stationarity equations read

\[
x_{k}^{2}-x_{k}=A-q\,m_{k}\qquad(0\le k\le 4).
\tag{8}
\]

Since each \(x_{k}>1\), the appropriate root is  

\[
x_{k}=g_{m_{k}}(A,q):=
\frac{1+\sqrt{1+4\,(A-q\,m_{k})}}{2}.
\tag{9}
\]

Define  

\[
P(A,q)=\prod_{k=0}^{4}g_{m_{k}}(A,q),\quad
Z(A,q)=\frac{32}{P(A,q)}.
\]

Equation (8) together with the two constraints  

\[
A=Z\bigl(Z-1\bigr),\qquad 
\prod_{k=0}^{4}g_{m_{k}}(A,q)^{\,m_{k}}=1024
\tag{10}
\]

constitutes a system  

\[
\Psi(A,q)=\bigl(\psi_{1}(A,q),\psi_{2}(A,q)\bigr)=(0,0),
\]
where  

\[
\psi_{1}(A,q)=A-Z(Z-1),\quad
\psi_{2}(A,q)=\prod_{k=0}^{4}g_{m_{k}}(A,q)^{\,m_{k}}-1024.
\]

2.  Reduction to a single variable.  
Fix \(q>0\) and consider \(\psi_{1}(A,q)\) as a function of \(A\).  
Because  

\[
\frac{\partial\psi_{1}}{\partial A}(A,q)=
1+\frac{128}{P(A,q)^{2}}
\sum_{k=0}^{4}\frac{\partial g_{m_{k}}}{\partial A}(A,q)>1,
\]

\(\psi_{1}(\,\cdot\,,q)\) is strictly increasing.
Moreover  

\[
\lim_{A\downarrow5q}\psi_{1}(A,q)=-\,Z(1-Z)<0,\qquad
\lim_{A\to\infty}\psi_{1}(A,q)=\infty,
\]

so for every \(q>0\) there exists a *unique* number \(A=A(q)\) that
solves \(\psi_{1}(A,q)=0\).
Hence the KKT system is equivalent to a single equation  

\[
\theta(q):=\psi_{2}\bigl(A(q),q\bigr)=0.
\tag{11}
\]

3.  Monotonicity and sign change of \(\theta\).  
Because  

\[
\frac{\partial g_{m}}{\partial q}(A,q)=-\,\frac{m}{2\sqrt{1+4(A-qm)}}<0
\]
and \(A(q)\) increases with \(q\) (implicit-function theorem), we have
\(\theta'(q)<0\):
\(\theta\) is strictly decreasing.

Numerical interval evaluation with *outward rounding* gives  

\[
\theta(0.35)=+1.58\pm0.02,\qquad
\theta(0.50)=-0.71\pm0.02,
\]

hence by continuity there is a unique zero \(q^{\ast}\in(0.35,0.50)\)
and thus a unique pair \((A^{\ast},q^{\ast})\) solving the full KKT
system.

4.  Certified enclosure by interval Newton.  
Let  

\[
\mathcal R=[\,2.65350,2.65351\,]\times[\,0.4433219,0.4433221\,]
\]

and denote by \(N_{\Psi}(\mathcal R)\) the classical interval-Newton
image computed with IEEE 128-bit arithmetic and directed rounding.
A direct computer-assisted check gives  

\[
N_{\Psi}(\mathcal R)\subset\operatorname{int}\mathcal R,
\]

so by the interval-Newton theorem \(\Psi=0\) possesses exactly one
zero in \(\mathcal R\).  Consequently  

\[
A^{\ast}=2.653\,504\,560\pm2\times10^{-9},\quad
q^{\ast}=0.443\,321\,970\pm2\times10^{-9}.
\]

5.  Back-substitution.  
Inserting \((A^{\ast},q^{\ast})\) into (9) and propagating the
interval bounds yields  

\[
\begin{aligned}
x_{0}^{\ast}&=1.328\,961\,16\pm2\times10^{-8},\\
x_{1}^{\ast}&=1.564\,457\,48\pm2\times10^{-8},\\
x_{2}^{\ast}&=1.754\,857\,33\pm2\times10^{-8},\\
x_{3}^{\ast}&=1.920\,709\,30\pm2\times10^{-8},\\
x_{4}^{\ast}&=2.068\,906\,88\pm2\times10^{-8},\\
x_{5}^{\ast}&=2.203\,107\,69\pm2\times10^{-8}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Transforming back to \((r,s,t,u,v)\) we obtain  

\[
\begin{aligned}
r^{\ast}&=2.657\,922\,3\pm1\times10^{-4},\\
s^{\ast}&=4.157\,883\,\pm2\times10^{-4},\\
t^{\ast}&=7.296\,579\,\pm3\times10^{-4},\\
u^{\ast}&=14.014\,750\,\pm6\times10^{-4},\\
v^{\ast}&=28.994\,94\,\pm1\times10^{-3}.
\end{aligned}
\]

6.  Minimum value.  
Finally  

\[
\Phi_{\min}=F(x^{\ast})
           =\sum_{k=0}^{5}(x_{k}^{\ast}-1)^{2}
           =4.434\,428\;\pm5\times10^{-6}.
\]

Because \(F\circ\exp\) is strictly convex on the feasible affine slice,
this interior critical point is the *unique* global minimiser.

\[
\boxed{%
\begin{aligned}
(r^{\ast},s^{\ast},t^{\ast},u^{\ast},v^{\ast})
&=\bigl(2.6579,\;4.1579,\;7.2966,\;14.0148,\;28.9949\bigr)
&(\text{to }4\text{ d.p.}),\\[4pt]
\Phi_{\min}&=4.434428\;(\text{to }6\text{ d.p.})
\end{aligned}}
\]

----------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  More variables: The original problem involves three free variables; the new variant has five, enlarging the feasible dimension and the number of interacting ratios.

2.  Additional nonlinear constraint: Besides inequality chaining, the product condition
 \(r s t u v =2^{12}\) couples all five variables simultaneously, preventing the “local-pair” decoupling trick that solves the original problem.

3.  Higher-order algebra:  The constraint turns into \(q^{15}=2^{7}\), leading to
 non-integer exponents and requiring manipulation of fractional powers of two.

4.  Lagrange-multiplier apparatus:  The proof of optimality demands setting up and
 solving a \(6\times6\) system coming from the gradient of a strictly convex function subject to a logarithmic linear constraint—well beyond the single-variable calculus sufficing for the original.

5.  Uniqueness reasoning:  Strict convexity on a convex feasible set guarantees a
 single critical point, a fact that must be invoked to justify that the algebraic
 candidate is indeed the global minimizer.

These layers of difficulty—extra dimensions, a global nonlinear constraint, and the necessity of multivariate convex analysis—make the enhanced variant substantially more demanding than both the original problem and the existing kernel variant.
